  • Suggested Readings

Week 7: Descriptive Statistics, EDA, and Hypothesis Testing

IA 240: Technology Applications in a Networked World

Section 1: Descriptive Statistics and Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

Before testing hypotheses, analysts must understand what their data looks like. Descriptive statistics and exploratory data analysis (EDA) are the systematic tools for that understanding — revealing distributions, central tendencies, variability, outliers, and relationships that formal models can miss or distort if applied without first examining the data.

Part I: Common Analytical Frameworks

Descriptive Analysis
Summarizes patterns, distributions, and central tendencies in the data. Answers: “What does this data look like?” Produces summary statistics, frequency tables, and basic visualizations. Always the starting point.
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
Goes beyond description to discover structure and relationships without a pre-specified hypothesis. Produces hypotheses for formal testing. Uses visualization extensively.
Inferential Analysis
Uses sample data to make inferences about a broader population. Involves hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, and probability distributions.
Predictive Modeling
Builds models that forecast future or unknown values. Includes regression (continuous outcomes), classification (categorical outcomes), and time series forecasting.
Causal Analysis
Attempts to establish cause-and-effect relationships, not just correlations. Requires careful attention to confounders, selection bias, and temporal ordering. Counterfactual reasoning is central.

Part II: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

What is EDA?

EDA is a critical step in the analysis process: systematically examining, visualizing, and summarizing data to uncover patterns, trends, relationships, and anomalies before applying formal models.

EDA serves four purposes:

  1. Understand data characteristics — distribution, central tendency, variability, variable relationships
  2. Detect errors and outliers — anomalies, missing values, data entry errors
  3. Guide analysis — inform choice of statistical methods; ensure models are appropriate
  4. Generate hypotheses — identify patterns worth testing formally

EDA Techniques

Summary Statistics (Descriptive Statistics)

Central Tendency:

Measure Definition Best for
Mean Sum of values divided by count; influenced by outliers Continuous, roughly symmetric data
Median Middle value when ordered; robust to outliers Skewed distributions, ordinal data
Mode Most frequently occurring value; data can be multimodal Categorical and discrete data

Dispersion:

Measure Definition Note
Range Maximum minus minimum Sensitive to outliers
Variance Average squared deviation from mean Units are squared
Standard Deviation Square root of variance; same units as data Most commonly reported
IQR 75th percentile minus 25th percentile (Q3 − Q1) Robust to outliers; used in box plots

Distribution Shape:

  • Skewness: Asymmetry of the distribution. Positive skew = long right tail (most values are low with some very high values—common in income, conflict casualties). Negative skew = long left tail.
  • Kurtosis: “Tailedness.” High kurtosis = heavy tails and a sharp peak (more extreme values than a normal distribution). Low kurtosis = light tails and flat peak.

EDA Visualization Techniques

Chart Type What It Reveals
Histogram Distribution shape, skewness, and spread for a single continuous variable
Box Plot Distribution, median, IQR, and outliers; useful for comparing groups
Scatter Plot Relationship between two continuous variables; reveals correlations, clusters, and nonlinearity
Bar Chart Frequency or proportion across categories
Line Graph Trends over time (time series)
Heatmap Correlations or patterns across many variables simultaneously

Identifying Outliers

Methods for detecting outliers:

  • Box plots: Points beyond the whiskers (typically 1.5× IQR from Q1 or Q3) are conventionally flagged
  • Z-scores: Data points more than 3 standard deviations from the mean are outliers in approximately normal distributions
  • Scatter plots: Visually identify points that deviate from the overall trend
WarningAre Outliers Errors or Signals?

Never automatically remove outliers. An outlier in a conflict event dataset might represent a genuine atrocity. An outlier in an economic dataset might represent a data entry error—or a genuine crisis. Investigate before deciding how to handle each case. Document your decision and its rationale.

Generating Hypotheses from EDA

EDA transitions analysis from description to explanation:

  1. Describe the observed pattern: “There is a positive relationship between hours studied and exam scores”
  2. Consider potential mechanisms: Why might this relationship exist? What underlying process produces it?
  3. Formulate a testable hypothesis: “Students who study for more than 10 hours per week achieve significantly higher exam scores than those who study fewer than 10 hours”
NoteSpurious Correlations — A Caution

EDA can produce misleading patterns. Correlations between unrelated time series are common when both trend over time. Visualizations can suggest relationships that are coincidental, not causal. Always ask: what mechanism could produce this pattern? If none exists, treat the correlation with skepticism. For memorable examples: tylervigen.com/spurious-correlations.

Section 2: Inferential Statistics and Hypothesis Testing

Descriptive statistics tell you what the data looks like. Inferential statistics let you draw conclusions about the world beyond your data — and evaluate whether observed patterns are likely to reflect genuine relationships or could plausibly have occurred by chance. This lecture covers the major statistical tests, the logic of hypothesis testing, and the critical limitations and failure modes of statistical inference that every analyst must internalize.

Part I: Handling Variable Types in Analysis

Variable Type Determines Method

Categorical (Nominal) Variables

Categories with no inherent order. Examples: country, vehicle type, party affiliation.

  • Descriptive: mode, frequency counts, percentages
  • Analysis: chi-square tests, logistic regression, log-linear models

Ordinal Variables

Ordered categories with unequal intervals. Examples: threat level, survey responses.

  • Descriptive: median, mode, percentiles
  • Analysis: non-parametric tests (Mann-Whitney U, Kruskal-Wallis), ordinal logistic regression

Interval Variables

Equal intervals, no true zero. Examples: temperature in Celsius, IQ scores, calendar years.

  • Descriptive: mean, standard deviation
  • Analysis: parametric tests (t-tests, ANOVA), correlation, linear regression

Ratio Variables

Equal intervals plus true zero. Examples: GDP, population, weight, casualties.

  • Descriptive: all central tendency and dispersion measures
  • Analysis: all parametric methods; logarithmic transformations often appropriate for right-skewed distributions

Continuous Variables

Can take any value in a range. Often ratio-scaled.

  • Correlation, regression, parametric tests

Part II: Statistical Tests for Hypothesis Testing

Overview

Statistical tests formalize hypothesis testing by asking: if the null hypothesis (no effect, no difference) were true, how likely would we observe data as extreme as what we found?

Test When to Use Key Assumption
z-test Large samples (n > 30); known population variance Normal distribution
t-test Small samples or unknown population variance Approximately normal data
Chi-square Categorical variables; tests for association or goodness of fit Independent observations; expected cell counts ≥ 5
ANOVA Comparing means across three or more groups Normal distribution; homogeneity of variance

Detailed Coverage

z-Test

Purpose: Determine whether a sample mean differs significantly from a known population mean (or compare two large sample means).

When to use: \(n > 30\) and population standard deviation is known or can be estimated from a large sample.

Example: A known average height of employees is 5’8” with standard deviation of 3”. A sample of 50 new hires has a mean height of 5’10”. Does this differ significantly from the employee population?

t-Test

Purpose: Compare means when sample size is small or population variance is unknown.

Types: - One-sample t-test: Compare one sample mean to a known value - Independent two-sample t-test: Compare means of two independent groups - Paired t-test: Compare means from the same group at two points in time

Example: Compare test scores before and after a training program (paired t-test, because the same individuals are measured twice).

Chi-Square Test

Purpose: Test for association between two categorical variables, or whether an observed frequency distribution matches an expected one.

Types: - Test for independence: Is there a statistically significant relationship between gender and product preference? - Goodness-of-fit test: Does the observed distribution of car colors match expected market preferences?

Key assumption: Expected cell frequencies ≥ 5 in each category.

ANOVA (Analysis of Variance)

Purpose: Compare means across three or more groups. More powerful than running multiple pairwise t-tests, which inflates Type I error (false positive) rates.

Types: - One-way ANOVA: One independent variable with three+ levels - Two-way ANOVA: Two independent variables; also tests for interaction effects

Example: Does the type of marketing campaign (Online, TV, Print) affect sales volume? A one-way ANOVA compares mean sales across all three campaign types simultaneously.

WarningA Note on Statistical Tests in Intelligence Analysis

Classic null hypothesis significance testing (NHST) was developed for experimental settings with comparable, randomly assigned groups—conditions that rarely hold in intelligence analysis. Be cautious about over-interpreting p-values when your “sample” is an entire population of states, or when your data comes from a non-random collection process. Effect size, confidence intervals, and substantive significance often matter more than the p-value.

Part III: Statistical Fallacies and Common Errors

Errors That Recur Across Intelligence and Research Contexts

Statistical analysis is not self-interpreting. Even correctly executed analyses can produce conclusions that are false, misleading, or analytically worthless if the analyst does not understand the limits of what the method can show.

Type I Error (False Positive)
Rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true — concluding that an effect exists when it does not. In intelligence: concluding that a pattern is significant (e.g., a state is developing a weapons program) when what was observed is statistical noise. The probability of a Type I error is controlled by the significance threshold α (typically 0.05). Intelligence consequence: Mobilizing resources, raising alert levels, or taking action based on a false signal — potentially with serious geopolitical consequences (recall the 2002 NIE on Iraqi WMD).
Type II Error (False Negative)
Failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is false — missing a real effect. In intelligence: failing to detect a weapons program or imminent attack that genuinely exists. Probability of Type II error is β; statistical power = 1 − β. Intelligence consequence: Failure to warn; strategic surprise. The tradeoff between Type I and Type II errors is a fundamental intelligence design choice — it is not possible to minimize both simultaneously with fixed data.
The Base Rate Fallacy
Ignoring the prior probability of an event when updating on new evidence. A test for a rare condition that is 99% accurate will produce mostly false positives if the condition is rare enough — because most tested individuals don’t have it. In intelligence: a signature (indicator) of terrorist planning may have high sensitivity but, because actual attacks are rare, most positive signals are false. Failing to account for base rates leads to systematic overconfidence in specific indicators.
Regression to the Mean
Extreme observations tend to be followed by less extreme observations — not because of any intervention but because extreme values are partly a product of chance. Classic example: pilots praised for excellent landings tend to perform worse next time; pilots criticized for poor landings tend to improve. Neither praise nor criticism caused the change — regression to the mean did. In intelligence: when a country’s conflict intensity is abnormally high in one year and drops the following year, that decline may reflect regression to the mean rather than the success of any intervention.
Ecological Fallacy
Inferring individual-level characteristics from group-level data. A country with high average corruption may contain many uncorrupt individuals; high poverty at the county level does not mean all residents of that county are poor. In intelligence: aggregate-level findings cannot be applied to individual cases without committing the ecological fallacy.
Simpson’s Paradox
A trend present in multiple groups of data disappears or reverses when the groups are combined. Classic example: a hospital appears to have lower survival rates than a smaller clinic when the data is combined — but within each category of patient severity, the hospital has better survival rates. The paradox arises because the groups differ in composition. Intelligence consequence: aggregate statistics can mask subgroup patterns that reverse the overall finding.

p-Values: What They Are and What They Are Not

The p-value is one of the most misunderstood quantities in applied statistics, and the misunderstanding has significant consequences for analytical reliability.

What a p-value is: > The probability of observing data as extreme or more extreme than what was observed, assuming the null hypothesis is true.

What a p-value is not:

  • NOT the probability that the null hypothesis is true
  • NOT the probability that the result occurred by chance
  • NOT the probability that the finding will replicate
  • NOT a measure of the size or importance of an effect
  • NOT proof that the null hypothesis is false when p < 0.05

The significance threshold problem:

The convention of α = 0.05 is arbitrary. Its origin is historical, not mathematical. A p-value of 0.049 is not meaningfully different from a p-value of 0.051 — but the first is declared “significant” and the second “not significant” under the threshold convention. In the intelligence context, where decisions are often binary (warn / don’t warn; act / don’t act), this threshold-sensitivity deserves serious analytical attention.

WarningThe 2002 Iraq NIE as a Case Study in Type I Error

The 2002 National Intelligence Estimate concluded with “high confidence” that Iraq possessed active WMD programs. This assessment rested on multiple indicators that were individually weak but collectively interpreted as confirming evidence. Multiple Type I errors — treating noise as signal, treating absence of disconfirming evidence as confirming evidence — compounded across the analytical process. The WMD Commission Report (2005) later identified the methodological failures: analysts had not considered the base rate (how often do states that appear to have WMD actually have them?), had not performed structured hypothesis testing against the null hypothesis (Iraq has no WMD), and had treated consistent-looking evidence as corroborating when the sources largely shared a common provenance.

Part IV: Correlation, Causation, and Confounding

The Most Important Distinction in Empirical Analysis

Identifying a correlation between two variables is a starting point, not a conclusion. The most consequential analytic error in empirical research is treating a demonstrated correlation as evidence of causation.

Three requirements for causal inference:

  1. Correlation: X and Y must covary
  2. Temporal precedence: X must precede Y in time
  3. Elimination of confounders: Alternative explanations (Z causes both X and Y) must be ruled out

Meeting all three requirements is methodologically demanding. Most observational data can meet the first and sometimes the second — but confounders are almost never fully eliminated without experimental design.

Illustrative intelligence case:

A study finds that countries with higher military spending have more civil conflicts. Does military spending cause conflict? Or:

  • Do conflict-prone countries (for reasons unrelated to military spending) spend more on their militaries? (Reverse causation)
  • Does political instability (a confounder) simultaneously increase military spending and conflict probability? (Common cause)
  • Is there a selection effect: are the countries with highest military spending concentrated in regions with pre-existing conflict drivers? (Selection bias)

Each of these alternative explanations would produce the observed correlation without any causal relationship between military spending and conflict.

TipTools for Causal Inference from Observational Data

Researchers have developed methods to approximate causal inference from observational data: instrumental variables (using a variable that predicts X but has no direct effect on Y); difference-in-differences (comparing before/after change within treatment and control groups); regression discontinuity (exploiting a threshold that assigns treatment quasi-randomly); and matching (pairing treated and control units on observable confounders). These methods are increasingly used in political science and econometrics but are rarely taught in intelligence training. Familiarity with their logic — and their limitations — is an asset for critically evaluating quantitative studies.

Suggested Readings

Nuzzo, R. (2014). Scientific method: Statistical errors. Nature, 506(7487), 150–152. https://doi.org/10.1038/506150a

A highly readable explanation of p-value misconceptions for a scientific generalist audience. Covers the false discovery rate problem, researcher degrees of freedom, and the distinction between statistical and practical significance.

Ioannidis, J. P. A. (2005). Why most published research findings are false. PLOS Medicine, 2(8), e124. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pmed.0020124

Demonstrates mathematically that under typical research conditions, a majority of statistically significant findings are false positives. A foundational paper for any analyst who uses published research as intelligence evidence.

Simmons, J. P., Nelson, L. D., & Simonsohn, U. (2011). False-positive psychology: Undisclosed flexibility in data collection and analysis allows presenting anything as significant. Psychological Science, 22(11), 1359–1366. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797611417632

Demonstrates that common, undisclosed analytical decisions dramatically inflate false-positive rates. Directly applicable to self-assessment of quantitative analytical choices.

Jervis, R. (2006). Reports, politics, and intelligence failures: The case of Iraq. Journal of Strategic Studies, 29(1), 3–52. https://doi.org/10.1080/01402390600566282

The 2002 Iraq WMD assessment failure as a case study in Type I error, confirmation bias, and failure to weight base rates. Shows what happens when inferential logic breaks down in applied intelligence production.

Donoho, D. (2017). 50 years of data science. Journal of Computational and Graphical Statistics, 26(4), 745–766. https://doi.org/10.1080/10618600.2017.1384734

A historical and conceptual overview of data science as a discipline. Places EDA in its intellectual genealogy — from Tukey’s foundational work through modern computational methods. Relevant background for understanding where descriptive and exploratory analysis fit in the broader analytical enterprise.

Provost, F., & Fawcett, T. (2013). Data science and its relationship to big data and data-driven decision making. Big Data, 1(1), 51–59. https://doi.org/10.1089/big.2013.1508

Examines the conceptual foundations of data-driven decision making and what distinguishes genuine data science from data mining. Directly relevant to the data science process and intelligence application content.

Davenport, T. H., & Patil, D. J. (2012). Data scientist: The sexiest job of the 21st century. Harvard Business Review, 90(10), 70–76.

Widely-read piece establishing the role of the data scientist in analytical organizations. Relevant to understanding the workflow and professional context in which the descriptive analysis skills of this lecture are deployed.
